question: Define the boundary of a set A in a metric space (X, d) and state the fundamental set-theoretic relationship between a set, its interior, and its boundary.
expectedPoints: The boundary (or frontier) of a set A, denoted by ∂A, is defined as the set of points x in X such that every open ball centered at x intersects both A and its complement, A^c. Formally, ∂A = { x ∈ X : for every ε > 0, B(x, ε) ∩ A ≠ ∅ and B(x, ε) ∩ A^c ≠ ∅ }.
The closure of A, denoted by cl(A), can be expressed as the union of the interior of A and the boundary of A: cl(A) = int(A) ∪ ∂A.
The interior of A, denoted by int(A), and the boundary of A are disjoint sets: int(A) ∩ ∂A = ∅.

question: Prove that the boundary of a set A in a metric space is equal to the closure of A minus its interior, i.e., ∂A = cl(A) \ int(A).
expectedPoints: Recall that the closure cl(A) is the union of all limit points of A, and the interior int(A) is the set of all points which are the center of some open ball contained entirely in A.

To show ∂A ⊆ cl(A) \ int(A): Take any x ∈ ∂A. By definition, every ball around x intersects A, so x is a limit point of A and thus x ∈ cl(A). Furthermore, x cannot be in int(A) because that would require a ball around x contained entirely in A, contradicting the condition that every ball around x also intersects A^c. Therefore, x ∈ cl(A) and x ∉ int(A), so x ∈ cl(A) \ int(A).

To show cl(A) \ int(A) ⊆ ∂A: Take any x ∈ cl(A) \ int(A). Since x ∈ cl(A), every ball around x intersects A. Since x ∉ int(A), no ball around x is entirely contained in A, meaning every ball around x must also intersect the complement A^c. Therefore, x satisfies the definition of being in ∂A.

question: Let Q be the set of rational numbers considered as a subset of the metric space R with the standard Euclidean metric. Determine the boundary of Q, ∂Q, and provide a rigorous justification for your answer, connecting it to the topological properties of Q and R.
expectedPoints: The boundary of Q in R is the entire real line, i.e., ∂Q = R.
Justification: For any real number x ∈ R and any ε > 0, the open interval (x - ε, x + ε) contains both rational numbers (since Q is dense in R) and irrational numbers (since R\Q is also dense in R).
Therefore, every ball B(x, ε) intersects both Q and its complement R\Q. This satisfies the definition of a boundary point for all x ∈ R.

Connection to Topology: This result demonstrates that Q has an empty interior (int(Q) = ∅) because any interval containing only rationals would be impossible due to the density of irrationals. Furthermore, the closure of Q is R (cl(Q) = R). Thus, the formula ∂Q = cl(Q) \ int(Q) = R \ ∅ = R holds consistently.
